name: Emirati Balaleet (Sweet Saffron Rice with Egg)
steps: Boil the noodles until tender, then toss with the sugar and cardamom.
Scramble the eggs separately and serve alongside.

ingredients_full.note: pinch, steeped in 1 tbsp hot water

steps_detailed: Boil the vermicelli 3-4 minutes until just tender, then drain well.
Melt the butter, stir in the sugar, cardamom, saffron and its water, and rosewater, then toss in the vermicelli until coated and lightly caramelized, 3-4 minutes.
Push the noodles to one side of the pan; scramble the eggs plain on the other side, keeping the two separate.
Serve side by side, scattered with pistachios - the plain egg is meant to be eaten in the same bite as the sweet noodles, not folded together into one mixture.

name: Emirati Chebab with Honey

ingredients.note: tbsp, standing in for date syrup (dibs)

steps: Whisk the flour, egg, and milk into a smooth, pourable batter.
Cook small rounds on a hot, lightly oiled griddle until bubbles form, then flip briefly.
Serve warm, drizzled with the honey.

ingredients_full.note: tbsp, standing in for date syrup (dibs)

steps_detailed: Whisk the flour, egg, milk, saffron, and cardamom into a smooth, pourable batter and rest 10 minutes.
Cook small, thick rounds on a hot, lightly oiled griddle, 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply golden on the bottom and set with a slightly spongy, honeycombed top.
Serve warm, drizzled generously with the honey - chebab, saffron-spiced pancakes, are an everyday Emirati breakfast, often paired with a strong cup of Arabic coffee.

name: Emirati Khameer Bread with Cheese
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, honey, a pinch of salt, and enough warm water to form a soft dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Pat into a round and cook on a hot, lightly oiled griddle until puffed and golden on both sides.
Split while warm and fill with the crumbled cheese.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, honey, a pinch of salt, the oil, and enough warm water to form a soft, slightly tacky dough. Cover and rest 25-30 minutes until slightly puffed.
Pat into a round about half an inch thick and cook on a hot, lightly oiled griddle, 3-4 minutes per side, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Split while still warm and fill with the crumbled cheese so it softens slightly - khameer, semi-sweet leavened bread, is a staple at Emirati breakfast tables, often served alongside honey or cheese.

name: Emirati Chicken Machboos
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the rice, tomatoes, turmeric, and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 20-22 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken in the oil, 8-10 minutes, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic in the same pot, 6-8 minutes, until deeply golden.
Add the tomatoes, cinnamon, cardamom, and pepper, and cook 3-4 minutes.
Return the chicken with 1.75 cups water, the pierced dried limes, and saffron, and simmer 20-25 minutes.
Lift out the chicken, stir the rice into the liquid, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes.
Fluff the rice and top with the chicken.

name: Emirati Fish Machboos
steps: Simmer the rice with the tomatoes and turmeric in 1.5 cups water, cover, and cook 20-22 minutes.
Pan-fry the fish separately and serve over the rice.
steps_detailed: Pan-fry the fish in a little of the oil until just cooked through, 3-4 minutes per side; set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic in the remaining oil, 6-8 minutes, until deeply golden.
Add the tomatoes, cinnamon, and cardamom, and cook 3-4 minutes.
Add 1.5 cups water, the pierced dried limes, and saffron, and bring to a simmer.
Stir in the rice, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes until tender.
Fluff the rice, top with the fish, and scatter with cilantro.

name: Emirati Cinnamon-Topped Harees
steps: Simmer the chicken in water until very tender, then shred it finely, reserving the broth.
Add the bulgur to the broth and simmer, mashing often, until it forms a thick, smooth porridge.
Stir the chicken back in and top with the ghee and cinnamon.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken in 2.5 cups water for 25-30 minutes until very tender, then shred it finely, reserving the broth.
Add the bulgur (standing in for wheat berries) to the broth and simmer 25-30 minutes, mashing frequently, until it forms a thick, smooth porridge.
Stir most of the shredded chicken back in, reserving a little to garnish, and top with the ghee, cinnamon, and a dusting of sugar.
This sweet-and-savory finish is a distinctly Emirati take on harees, especially popular during Ramadan and at weddings.

name: Emirati Margooga
steps: Simmer the chicken with the potato and tomatoes until tender, about 20-25 minutes, mashing some of the potato to thicken.
Stir the torn bread through the stew and let it soften and thicken for a few minutes before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken with the potato, tomatoes, onion, and cumin in a splash of water for 25-30 minutes until tender, mashing some of the potato against the side of the pot to thicken the base.
Stir the torn bread directly into the stew and let it simmer a few more minutes, breaking down and thickening the whole dish into a hearty, porridge-like consistency.
Margooga, thicker and heartier than thareed, is a comforting, everyday Emirati dish built to stretch a little meat and bread a long way.

name: Emirati Ghuzi (Spiced Lamb over Rice)
steps: Brown the lamb with the onion and cardamom.
Add the rice and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 20-22 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: formed into a few large chunks

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the onion in the oil, 9-11 minutes.
Add the cardamom, cinnamon, saffron, and 2 cups water, cover, and simmer 35-40 minutes until very tender.
Lift out the lamb, stir the rice into the liquid, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes.
Toast the almonds 2-3 minutes until golden.
Mound the rice, top with the lamb, scatter with almonds and raisins.

name: Emirati Lamb Machboos
steps: Brown the lamb with the onion.
Add the rice, tomatoes, turmeric, and 1.5 cups water, cover, and simmer 20-22 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: formed into a few large chunks

steps_detailed: Brown the lamb in the oil, 9-11 minutes, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic, 6-8 minutes, until deeply golden.
Add the tomatoes, cinnamon, cardamom, and nutmeg, and cook 3-4 minutes.
Return the lamb with 2 cups water, the pierced dried limes, and saffron, cover, and simmer 40-45 minutes until very tender.
Lift out the lamb, stir the rice into the liquid, cover, and cook over low heat 18-20 minutes.
Fluff the rice and top with the lamb.

name: Emirati Chicken Saloona
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the potato, tomatoes, and 1 cup water, and simmer 25-30 minutes until the potato is tender.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, cumin, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the potato, diced tomatoes, and 1.25 cups water, and simmer 28-32 minutes until the potato is fully tender and the broth has thickened slightly.
Saloona, a loose, everyday vegetable-and-meat stew ladled over rice, is a weeknight staple in Emirati homes, endlessly adaptable to whatever vegetables are on hand.

name: Emirati Spiced Grilled Fish with Lime Rice
steps: Marinate the fish in the lime juice and garlic for 15 minutes, then grill or pan-fry, 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve over the cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the fish in the lime juice, garlic, cumin, and paprika for at least 15 minutes.
Grill or pan-fry over high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until just cooked through and lightly charred.

Cook the basmati in 1.25 cups water, covered, 15-18 minutes, until tender and fully separate - basmati's dry, distinct grains matter here since the rice is served plain alongside the fish rather than cooked into it.
Fluff the rice, finish with a little more lime juice and the cilantro, and serve alongside the fish.

name: Emirati Samboosa
steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Make a simple dough from the flour and water, roll thin, cut into strips, fill, and fold into triangles.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, cumin, and cilantro.
Make a simple, firm dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into strips, fill with the beef mixture, and fold into tight triangles, sealing the edges well.
Fry until deeply golden and crisp, 4-5 minutes - samboosa are a fixture at Emirati gatherings, appearing in enormous batches during Ramadan iftar spreads.

name: Emirati Khubz with Cheese and Za'atar
steps: Warm the bread on a dry pan.
Scatter with the cheese and za'atar, and drizzle with the oil.
Fold and serve warm.
steps_detailed: Warm the bread on a dry pan over medium heat until softened and lightly speckled, 1-2 minutes per side.
Scatter generously with the crumbled cheese and za'atar, and drizzle with the oil.
Fold in half and press briefly on the pan until the cheese just softens - a simple, common everyday snack sold from bakeries and corner shops across the UAE.

name: Emirati Regag-Style Cheese and Egg Crepe
steps: Whisk the flour with enough water to make a very thin, pourable batter.
Spread thin on a hot, oiled pan, crack the egg over it, and scatter with the cheese.
Cook until set, fold, and serve.
steps_detailed: Whisk the flour with enough water to make a very thin, almost lacy batter, similar to a crepe.
Spread thin across a hot, well-oiled pan, crack the egg directly over it, season with black pepper, and scatter with the cheese.

Cook 2-3 minutes until the edges crisp and lift and the egg sets, then fold into quarters and serve warm - regag, paper-thin and often sold from street carts, is one of the UAE's most iconic snack foods.

name: Emirati Za'atar Hummus

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, and lime juice with a splash of water until smooth.
Top with the za'atar and serve with flatbread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, and garlic with a splash of water until very smooth.
Spoon into a shallow dish, drizzle with the oil, and top generously with the za'atar - hummus finished this way is a common mezze offering at Emirati gatherings alongside warm bread.

name: Emirati Luqaimat

ingredients.note: tbsp, standing in for date syrup

steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, and enough water to form a thick, smooth batter. Rest 20 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until puffed and golden.
Drizzle with the honey while still warm.

ingredients_full.note: tbsp, standing in for date syrup

steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, saffron, and enough warm water to form a thick, smooth, pourable batter. Cover and rest 25-30 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry, turning occasionally, 3-5 minutes until puffed, deeply golden, and cooked through.
Drain briefly and drizzle generously with the honey while still warm - luqaimat are the UAE's most iconic sweet, ubiquitous at Ramadan iftars and family gatherings alike.

name: Emirati Khabees (Date and Ghee Pudding)
steps: Toast the flour in the ghee until lightly golden and fragrant.
Stir in the mashed dates, sugar, and a splash of water, and cook until it comes together into a thick, spoonable paste.
steps_detailed: Toast the flour in the ghee over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, 4-5 minutes until lightly golden and nutty-smelling.
Stir in the mashed dates, sugar, cardamom, and a generous splash of water, and cook 5-7 more minutes, stirring, until it comes together into a thick, dark, spoonable paste.
Khabees, a rich date-and-flour pudding, is a traditional Emirati sweet often prepared for special occasions and shared generously among guests.

name: Emirati Milk-Soaked Bread Pudding with Pistachios
steps: Warm the milk with the sugar, rosewater, and saffron over low heat until the sugar dissolves and the milk turns a pale gold, 3-4 minutes.
Pour over the torn bread in a small dish and let it soak 10-15 minutes until soft and fully absorbed.
Top generously with the pistachios and serve - milk-soaked bread desserts scented with rosewater and saffron are a gentle, comforting end to an Emirati meal.
steps_detailed: Warm the milk with the sugar, rosewater, and saffron over low heat until the sugar dissolves and the milk turns a pale gold, 3-4 minutes.
Pour over the torn bread in a small dish and let it soak 10-15 minutes until soft and fully absorbed.
Top generously with the pistachios and serve - milk-soaked bread desserts scented with rosewater and saffron are a gentle, comforting end to an Emirati meal.

Real sliced bread is used here now - a tortilla's texture and how it soaks up liquid are both quite different from actual bread, which matters a lot in a dish where the bread itself is doing real work (soaking, thickening, or being the sandwich structure), not just serving as a flat wrapper.

name: Emirati Tomato Lime Dipping Sauce
steps: Mash the tomatoes lightly and combine with the lime juice, garlic, and cilantro.
Serve alongside grilled meats or fish.
steps_detailed: Mash the tomatoes lightly and combine with the lime juice, garlic, cilantro, and jalapeno.
Let sit 10 minutes so the flavors meld - a bright, fresh dip that pairs especially well with grilled fish or the spiced grilled fish machboos-style plates common at Emirati family lunches.

name: Emirati Date and Ghee Drizzle
steps: Blend the dates with the melted ghee and a splash of warm water until smooth and pourable.
Drizzle over bread, pancakes, or fresh fruit.
steps_detailed: Blend the dates with the melted ghee, rosewater, and a splash of warm water until smooth and pourable.
Drizzle over khameer, chebab, or fresh fruit - rich date-and-ghee condiments like this one are woven through Emirati hospitality, always offered generously to guests.
